text: My daughter died. It’ll be twenty-eight years this month. The thirtieth of this month, yeah. She died at Methodist Hospital. She had an operation for appendicitis. It was late Saturday afternoon when they operated, very late. Sunday morning, my oldest daughter, Victoria, went to the hospital to see how she was. She was still under ether. Back then, they used a lot of ether. There was a chair there. They were just starting to get people out of bed, you know. So, Victoria saw the chair, and she said to the nurse, “What’s this chair doing here?” The nurse said, “Well, we’ve got to get her out of bed.” She said, “What are you talking about? She’s still under ether.” “She’s too sick.” But the nurse said, “Well, that’s the doctor’s orders.” So, they went to get her out of bed, and she passed out, and they had to put her back in bed. See, here, she never – I don’t know if they knew or what, but I blame them for the blood clot she got, taking her out of bed. So, when she was walking around to come home after ten days, I was waiting for the call from the hospital. I didn’t have a phone then. The lady from the store who had a phone called me, “Mrs. Costa,” at aquarter to five at night. “Mrs. Costa, they just called you from the hospital. Go Pick Marie up. She’s discharged.” I had all the clothes laid out on the table. And Rita was – I was cooking, I said, “Rita, finish the cooking.” Daddy will be home soon.” He was working down in the yard. I said, “And put a big pot of coffee on,” because she had been wishing for coffee. And you know, every morning, that first cup of coffee, I offered it to her. When I get up in the morning, the first cup is hers. Since she’s gone, I never forget that. So I said, “Put a pot of coffee on, and send for some cake from the bakery.” I’m going to get Marie; they called me.” “All right, mother, go ahead,” she said, “I’ll finish up.” And I went myself. You know, I had the coat on my arm. She was operated on in March – No, April! So that was the thirtieth. She was operated – ten days before, so that would be the twentieth. The twentieth of April, right? It was kind of chilly. Oh, and I had everything in a bag, and I had the coat on my arm and her dress hanging on my arm. She saw me from the windows, from Wolf Street. She said, “My mother’s just coming.” She was with the girls, saying goodbye to everyone – you know how it is. They told me on the phone, it would be ten dollars more for her board. And to make sure I brought her girdle to put on. I said, “All right, I’ll be right over.” And I went. But when I got there, the nurse said to me, “Sit down, Mrs. Costa, sit here for a while.” So I sat down, and I was praying for another lady in the hospital that had an operation for her gall bladder. She lived near my sister, and she had ten children. They had said she was in very bad shape. I prayed, “Oh dear God, don’t take that mother away from her children.” I was praying for her, you know. Then a little nurse’s aide went to the closet and got some blankets. I started getting the chills, and I was thinking about it, when the aide said to the nurse, “Oh, she’s going into a cold sweat now.” The nurse hung up the phone and came to me. She said, “Mrs. Costa, has your daughter ever had a fainting spell?” “Oh my God!” I said, “Don’t tell me you’re running for my daughter!” “Yes,” she said, “come on in.” So, I went into the room. And my daughter was on the bed with her bedroom slippers and her housecoat on. And the beads of sweat – honest to God, not to exaggerate, they were that big. It came – just poured from her – I said, “What did you do, kill my daughter? You killed her! You killed her!” That’s what I kept yelling. And the doctor rushed in, see, they were trying to get the doctor, you know. She was a lady doctor. Dr. Schwartz actually did the operation, but she was his assistant, Dr. Montique. She was a lady doctor. So, she came in and she said – I said, “You killed my daughter – what did you do to her?” She said, “We don’t know what happened, Mrs. Costa.” She took me away from the bed. The nurse said to me, “Talk to her.” I said, “Talk to her? My daughter’s dying, what am I supposed to tell her?” And I was screaming. My hair was all pulled out. I went hysterical, you know what I mean? She was only eighteen years old. Beautiful girl, you can see from her picture. And just as I turned my back, my daughter passed away. She was getting ready to go home. I had the clothes and everything ready. My husband was waiting for her. He came home, went upstairs to shave and wash, then sat on the step, waiting. “When is the mother coming?” The hospital kept calling. The people in the office didn’t know what had happened yet. They kept calling the store, trying to reach us. They asked, “When is the mother coming to pick up Marie? She’s discharged.” And Rita said “That’s strange, the mother went to the hospital. She should be there.” So they came back to the hospital to look for us. Rita said, “Gee, that’s strange,” she said, “Mother must’ve gotten really worked up about Marie.” You know, I’ve always had a bad heart. “Mother must’ve gotten so upset about Marie, she probably made herself sick,” Rita thought. So she came over with my son-in-law, in the car. And I was in the main hallway, at the office. I was all pulled apart. One doctor wanted to give me a shot. I said, “Get out of here, I’ll kick you,” I said. “You killed my daughter, now you want to kill me?” Another nurse came over. See, they all knew me. I had three operations in no time, not even a year. And I knew all the nurses. “Mrs. Costa, please, take –” “Get out!” I said, “Don’t you dare come near me.” I was so hysterical. I was banging my head against the wall and everything. They had to grab me. Then they brought me to the office, see. When Rita came in and saw me like that, she asked, “Mother, what happened?” I said, “We lost Marie. Marie died.” Well, that’s this one. This is a picture of Rita. She went down like lightning. Dropped to the floor, and that’s a marble floor. She went down like lightning. And the back of her leg, it felt just like a pole. Hard like a pole, the back of her leg. And that girl is suffering. Thirty – no, twenty-eight years since her sister died? When she gets that pain, it’s like she has phlebitis from it. She suffers – seven, eight, nine weeks at a time. She can’t walk, she goes through such pain since then. And how did they figure how Marie died? The girl who was walking with Marie, told the doctor that Marie was saying goodbye to Arlene. And all Marie said to that girl was, “Oh, I’ve got a pain in my leg and I can’t see.” And then Marie collapsed. Later, they said it was a blood clot. I said, “Don’t you dare touch my daughter. I’ll burn this hospital down! I don’t care who’s in it.” I said, “Don’t you dare touch my daughter. You want to find out what she died from.” But if you touch my daughter, I’ll kill you.” I wouldn’t let them touch her. So when I got home – they took me home. I was screaming in the car. I couldn’t get out, you know, I couldn’t – I was screaming! Everybody came out, coming out of their doors because they could hear the screams. My husband was sitting on the step. He asked, “What’s the matter? What happened to you?” Well, my hair was all pulled, and my face – I was a mess. I didn’t know what I had done to myself. I said, “Mike, we lost Marie. Marie died.” It was just a shock. My husband had a heart attack. Yes, he had a heart attack. Then my son, Michael, yeah. That night. Right there, my Michael came, and my Michael collapsed. He had a heart attack too, no kidding. It was all – my two sisters were here, waiting until about half-past four. They waited because they knew she was coming home that day, waiting for the call. My younger sister had brought an orange for Marie, and I had never seen an orange like it. It was a big orange, like that. She said, “Mary, look, when they call you, when you pick Marie up, let us know, and we’ll come over.” I said, “All right.” So, I got home and called everybody, told them what happened. I called my brother-in-law. He was an undertaker. My sister’s husband – he’s passed away now too. God rest his soul. Maybe you know him... So they were waiting for my call, you know, but instead, I sent my son James, he was little then. I said, “Go tell Aunt Millie and Aunt Jenny.” They had cleaned the pavement, and done the cement work, and they were cleaning the pavement. They said, “Here comes little Jamie,” and now they said, “Maybe he’s come to tell us Marie’s home.” But when he got there, the poor kid’s eyes were all red. They said, “James, what’s the matter, honey? Why are you crying?” “Aunt Jenny, Aunt Millie, Marie died,” he said. Well, my sisters and my mother, you know, they were all alive. It was a shock to everybody. That was a shock that – The neighborhood – Annie at the store always says, “Mrs. Costa,” every time I go in there, she reminds me, “I just picture Marie standing at that step, looking over here.” She used to stand at that step. She was a real quiet girl, pleasant, didn’t talk much, but she was so good, I can’t even tell you. That’s all. So that’s what happened.
summary: My daughter died. It’ll be twenty-eight years this month. She had an operation for appendicitis. I blame them for the blood clot she got, taking her out of bed. And just as I turned my back, my daughter passed away. Later, they said it was a blood clot. My husband had a heart attack.

summary: My daughter Marie died twenty-eight years ago after an operation for appendicitis. I blame the hospital for taking her out of bed too soon, which caused a blood clot. I went to the hospital to bring Marie home, but she suddenly became very ill. I was devastated and went hysterical when Marie died. My family suffered greatly after Marie’s death, with my husband and son both having heart attacks. I informed my relatives and neighbors about Marie’s death, and everyone was shocked. I still remember and miss Marie every day.

I said, “And put a big pot of coffee on,” because she had been wishing for coffee. And you know, every morning, that first cup of coffee, I offered it to her. When I get up in the morning, the first cup is hers. Since she’s gone, I never forget that. So I said, “Put a pot of coffee on, and send for some cake from the bakery.” I’m going to get Marie; they called me.” “All right, mother, go ahead,” she said, “I’ll finish up.” And I went myself. You know, I had the coat on my arm. She was operated on in March – No, April! So that was the thirtieth. She was operated – ten days before, so that would be the twentieth. The twentieth of April, right? It was kind of chilly. Oh, and I had everything in a bag, and I had the coat on my arm and her dress hanging on my arm. She saw me from the windows, from Wolf Street. She said, “My mother’s just coming.” She was with the girls, saying goodbye to everyone – you know how it is. They told me on the phone, it would be ten dollars more for her board. And to make sure I brought her girdle to put on. I said, “All right, I’ll be right over.” And I went. But when I got there, the nurse said to me, “Sit down, Mrs. Costa, sit here for a while.” So I sat down, and I was praying for another lady in the hospital that had an operation for her gall bladder. She lived near my sister, and she had ten children. They had said she was in very bad shape. I prayed, “Oh dear God, don’t take that mother away from her children.” I was praying for her, you know. Then a little nurse’s aide went to the closet and got some blankets. I started getting the chills, and I was thinking about it, when the aide said to the nurse, “Oh, she’s going into a cold sweat now.” The nurse hung up the phone and came to me. She said, “Mrs. Costa, has your daughter ever had a fainting spell?” “Oh my God!” I said, “Don’t tell me you’re running for my daughter!” “Yes,” she said, “come on in.” So, I went into the room. And my daughter was on the bed with her bedroom slippers and her housecoat on. And the beads of sweat – honest to God, not to exaggerate, they were that big. It came – just poured from her – I said, “What did you do, kill my daughter? You killed her! You killed her!” That’s what I kept yelling. And the doctor rushed in, see, they were trying to get the doctor, you know. She was a lady doctor. Dr. Schwartz actually did the operation, but she was his assistant, Dr. Montique. She was a lady doctor. So, she came in and she said – I said, “You killed my daughter – what did you do to her?” She said, “We don’t know what happened, Mrs. Costa.” She took me away from the bed. The nurse said to me, “Talk to her.” I said, “Talk to her? My daughter’s dying, what am I supposed to tell her?” And I was screaming. My hair was all pulled out. I went hysterical, you know what I mean? She was only eighteen years old. Beautiful girl, you can see from her picture. And just as I turned my back, my daughter passed away. She was getting ready to go home. I had the clothes and everything ready. My husband was waiting for her. He came home, went upstairs to shave and wash, then sat on the step, waiting. “When is the mother coming?” The hospital kept calling. The people in the office didn’t know what had happened yet. They kept calling the store, trying to reach us. They asked, “When is the mother coming to pick up Marie? She’s discharged.” And Rita said “That’s strange, the mother went to the hospital. She should be there.” So they came back to the hospital to look for us. Rita said, “Gee, that’s strange,” she said, “Mother must’ve gotten really worked up about Marie.” You know, I’ve always had a bad heart. “Mother must’ve gotten so upset about Marie, she probably made herself sick,” Rita thought. So she came over with my son-in-law, in the car. And I was in the main hallway, at the office. I was all pulled apart. One doctor wanted to give me a shot. I said, “Get out of here, I’ll kick you,” I said. “You killed my daughter, now you want to kill me?” Another nurse came over. See, they all knew me. I had three operations in no time, not even a year. And I knew all the nurses. “Mrs. Costa, please, take –” “Get out!” I said, “Don’t you dare come near me.” I was so hysterical. I was banging my head against the wall and everything. They had to grab me. Then they brought me to the office, see. When Rita came in and saw me like that, she asked, “Mother, what happened?” I said, “We lost Marie. Marie died.” Well, that’s this one. This is a picture of Rita. She went down like lightning. Dropped to the floor, and that’s a marble floor. She went down like lightning. And the back of her leg, it felt just like a pole. Hard like a pole, the back of her leg. And that girl is suffering. Thirty – no, twenty-eight years since her sister died? When she gets that pain, it’s like she has phlebitis from it. She suffers – seven, eight, nine weeks at a time. She can’t walk, she goes through such pain since then. And how did they figure how Marie died? The girl who was walking with Marie, told the doctor that Marie was saying goodbye to Arlene. And all Marie said to that girl was, “Oh, I’ve got a pain in my leg and I can’t see.” And then Marie collapsed. Later, they said it was a blood clot. I said, “Don’t you dare touch my daughter. I’ll burn this hospital down! I don’t care who’s in it.” I said, “Don’t you dare touch my daughter. You want to find out what she died from.” But if you touch my daughter, I’ll kill you.” I wouldn’t let them touch her. So when I got home – they took me home. I was screaming in the car. I couldn’t get out, you know, I couldn’t – I was screaming! Everybody came out, coming out of their doors because they could hear the screams. My husband was sitting on the step. He asked, “What’s the matter? What happened to you?” Well, my hair was all pulled, and my face – I was a mess. I didn’t know what I had done to myself. I said, “Mike, we lost Marie. Marie died.” It was just a shock. My husband had a heart attack. Yes, he had a heart attack. Then my son, Michael, yeah. That night. Right there, my Michael came, and my Michael collapsed. He had a heart attack too, no kidding. It was all – my two sisters were here, waiting until about half-past four. They waited because they knew she was coming home that day, waiting for the call. My younger sister had brought an orange for Marie, and I had never seen an orange like it. It was a big orange, like that. She said, “Mary, look, when they call you, when you pick Marie up, let us know, and we’ll come over.” I said, “All right.” So, I got home and called everybody, told them what happened. I called my brother-in-law. He was an undertaker. My sister’s husband – he’s passed away now too. God rest his soul. Maybe you know him... So they were waiting for my call, you know, but instead, I sent my son James, he was little then. I said, “Go tell Aunt Millie and Aunt Jenny.” They had cleaned the pavement, and done the cement work, and they were cleaning the pavement. They said, “Here comes little Jamie,” and now they said, “Maybe he’s come to tell us Marie’s home.” But when he got there, the poor kid’s eyes were all red. They said, “James, what’s the matter, honey? Why are you crying?” “Aunt Jenny, Aunt Millie, Marie died,” he said. Well, my sisters and my mother, you know, they were all alive. It was a shock to everybody. That was a shock that – The neighborhood – Annie at the store always says, “Mrs. Costa,” every time I go in there, she reminds me, “I just picture Marie standing at that step, looking over here.” She used to stand at that step. She was a real quiet girl, pleasant, didn’t talk much, but she was so good, I can’t even tell you. That’s all. So that’s what happened.

She’s still under ether.” “She’s too sick.” But the nurse said, “Well, that’s the doctor’s orders.” So, they went to get her out of bed, and she passed out, and they had to put her back in bed. See, here, she never – I don’t know if they knew or what, but I blame them for the blood clot she got, taking her out of bed. So, when she was walking around to come home after ten days, I was waiting for the call from the hospital. I didn’t have a phone then. The lady from the store who had a phone called me, “Mrs. Costa,” at aquarter to five at night. “Mrs. Costa, they just called you from the hospital. Go Pick Marie up. She’s discharged.” I had all the clothes laid out on the table. And Rita was – I was cooking, I said, “Rita, finish the cooking.” Daddy will be home soon.” He was working down in the yard. I said, “And put a big pot of coffee on,” because she had been wishing for coffee. And you know, every morning, that first cup of coffee, I offered it to her. When I get up in the morning, the first cup is hers. Since she’s gone, I never forget that. So I said, “Put a pot of coffee on, and send for some cake from the bakery.” I’m going to get Marie; they called me.” “All right, mother, go ahead,” she said, “I’ll finish up.” And I went myself. You know, I had the coat on my arm. She was operated on in March – No, April! So that was the thirtieth. She was operated – ten days before, so that would be the twentieth. The twentieth of April, right? It was kind of chilly. Oh, and I had everything in a bag, and I had the coat on my arm and her dress hanging on my arm. She saw me from the windows, from Wolf Street. She said, “My mother’s just coming.” She was with the girls, saying goodbye to everyone – you know how it is. They told me on the phone, it would be ten dollars more for her board. And to make sure I brought her girdle to put on. I said, “All right, I’ll be right over.” And I went. But when I got there, the nurse said to me, “Sit down, Mrs. Costa, sit here for a while.” So I sat down, and I was praying for another lady in the hospital that had an operation for her gall bladder. She lived near my sister, and she had ten children. They had said she was in very bad shape. I prayed, “Oh dear God, don’t take that mother away from her children.” I was praying for her, you know. Then a little nurse’s aide went to the closet and got some blankets. I started getting the chills, and I was thinking about it, when the aide said to the nurse, “Oh, she’s going into a cold sweat now.” The nurse hung up the phone and came to me. She said, “Mrs. Costa, has your daughter ever had a fainting spell?” “Oh my God!” I said, “Don’t tell me you’re running for my daughter!” “Yes,” she said, “come on in.” So, I went into the room. And my daughter was on the bed with her bedroom slippers and her housecoat on. And the beads of sweat – honest to God, not to exaggerate, they were that big. It came – just poured from her – I said, “What did you do, kill my daughter? You killed her! You killed her!” That’s what I kept yelling. And the doctor rushed in, see, they were trying to get the doctor, you know. She was a lady doctor. Dr. Schwartz actually did the operation, but she was his assistant, Dr. Montique. She was a lady doctor. So, she came in and she said – I said, “You killed my daughter – what did you do to her?” She said, “We don’t know what happened, Mrs. Costa.” She took me away from the bed. The nurse said to me, “Talk to her.” I said, “Talk to her? My daughter’s dying, what am I supposed to tell her?” And I was screaming. My hair was all pulled out. I went hysterical, you know what I mean? She was only eighteen years old. Beautiful girl, you can see from her picture. And just as I turned my back, my daughter passed away. She was getting ready to go home. I had the clothes and everything ready. My husband was waiting for her. He came home, went upstairs to shave and wash, then sat on the step, waiting. “When is the mother coming?” The hospital kept calling. The people in the office didn’t know what had happened yet. They kept calling the store, trying to reach us. They asked, “When is the mother coming to pick up Marie? She’s discharged.” And Rita said “That’s strange, the mother went to the hospital. She should be there.” So they came back to the hospital to look for us. Rita said, “Gee, that’s strange,” she said, “Mother must’ve gotten really worked up about Marie.” You know, I’ve always had a bad heart. “Mother must’ve gotten so upset about Marie, she probably made herself sick,” Rita thought. So she came over with my son-in-law, in the car. And I was in the main hallway, at the office. I was all pulled apart. One doctor wanted to give me a shot. I said, “Get out of here, I’ll kick you,” I said. “You killed my daughter, now you want to kill me?” Another nurse came over. See, they all knew me. I had three operations in no time, not even a year. And I knew all the nurses. “Mrs. Costa, please, take –” “Get out!” I said, “Don’t you dare come near me.” I was so hysterical. I was banging my head against the wall and everything. They had to grab me. Then they brought me to the office, see. When Rita came in and saw me like that, she asked, “Mother, what happened?” I said, “We lost Marie. Marie died.” Well, that’s this one. This is a picture of Rita. She went down like lightning. Dropped to the floor, and that’s a marble floor. She went down like lightning. And the back of her leg, it felt just like a pole. Hard like a pole, the back of her leg. And that girl is suffering. Thirty – no, twenty-eight years since her sister died? When she gets that pain, it’s like she has phlebitis from it. She suffers – seven, eight, nine weeks at a time. She can’t walk, she goes through such pain since then. And how did they figure how Marie died? The girl who was walking with Marie, told the doctor that Marie was saying goodbye to Arlene. And all Marie said to that girl was, “Oh, I’ve got a pain in my leg and I can’t see.” And then Marie collapsed. Later, they said it was a blood clot. I said, “Don’t you dare touch my daughter. I’ll burn this hospital down! I don’t care who’s in it.” I said, “Don’t you dare touch my daughter. You want to find out what she died from.” But if you touch my daughter, I’ll kill you.” I wouldn’t let them touch her. So when I got home – they took me home. I was screaming in the car. I couldn’t get out, you know, I couldn’t – I was screaming! Everybody came out, coming out of their doors because they could hear the screams. My husband was sitting on the step. He asked, “What’s the matter? What happened to you?” Well, my hair was all pulled, and my face – I was a mess. I didn’t know what I had done to myself. I said, “Mike, we lost Marie. Marie died.” It was just a shock. My husband had a heart attack. Yes, he had a heart attack. Then my son, Michael, yeah. That night. Right there, my Michael came, and my Michael collapsed. He had a heart attack too, no kidding. It was all – my two sisters were here, waiting until about half-past four. They waited because they knew she was coming home that day, waiting for the call. My younger sister had brought an orange for Marie, and I had never seen an orange like it. It was a big orange, like that. She said, “Mary, look, when they call you, when you pick Marie up, let us know, and we’ll come over.” I said, “All right.” So, I got home and called everybody, told them what happened. I called my brother-in-law. He was an undertaker. My sister’s husband – he’s passed away now too. God rest his soul. Maybe you know him... So they were waiting for my call, you know, but instead, I sent my son James, he was little then. I said, “Go tell Aunt Millie and Aunt Jenny.” They had cleaned the pavement, and done the cement work, and they were cleaning the pavement. They said, “Here comes little Jamie,” and now they said, “Maybe he’s come to tell us Marie’s home.” But when he got there, the poor kid’s eyes were all red. They said, “James, what’s the matter, honey? Why are you crying?” “Aunt Jenny, Aunt Millie, Marie died,” he said. Well, my sisters and my mother, you know, they were all alive. It was a shock to everybody. That was a shock that – The neighborhood – Annie at the store always says, “Mrs. Costa,” every time I go in there, she reminds me, “I just picture Marie standing at that step, looking over here.” She used to stand at that step. She was a real quiet girl, pleasant, didn’t talk much, but she was so good, I can’t even tell you. That’s all. So that’s what happened.
summary: My daughter died twenty-eight years ago this month. She died at Methodist Hospital. She had an operation for appendicitis. The operation was late on a Saturday afternoon. My oldest daughter Victoria visited her the next morning. My daughter was still under ether when Victoria visited. The nurse tried to get her out of bed too soon. My daughter passed out when they tried to move her. I blame the hospital for the blood clot she got. After ten days, the hospital called to say she could come home. I prepared her clothes and food for her return. I always offered her the first cup of coffee in the morning. I went to the hospital to pick her up. The nurse told me to sit and wait when I arrived. I prayed for another sick woman while waiting. I started feeling chills and a cold sweat. The nurse asked if my daughter ever fainted. I was brought to my daughter’s room. My daughter was sweating heavily and dying. I accused the hospital staff of killing my daughter. The doctor said they didn’t know what happened. My daughter died as I turned my back. My husband was waiting at home for her. My family was shocked and devastated by her death. My husband and son both had heart attacks that night. I told my family and neighbors about Marie’s death. The whole neighborhood was shocked and remembered Marie fondly.

The twentieth of April, right? It was kind of chilly. Oh, and I had everything in a bag, and I had the coat on my arm and her dress hanging on my arm. She saw me from the windows, from Wolf Street. She said, “My mother’s just coming.” She was with the girls, saying goodbye to everyone – you know how it is. They told me on the phone, it would be ten dollars more for her board. And to make sure I brought her girdle to put on. I said, “All right, I’ll be right over.” And I went. But when I got there, the nurse said to me, “Sit down, Mrs. Costa, sit here for a while.” So I sat down, and I was praying for another lady in the hospital that had an operation for her gall bladder. She lived near my sister, and she had ten children. They had said she was in very bad shape. I prayed, “Oh dear God, don’t take that mother away from her children.” I was praying for her, you know. Then a little nurse’s aide went to the closet and got some blankets. I started getting the chills, and I was thinking about it, when the aide said to the nurse, “Oh, she’s going into a cold sweat now.” The nurse hung up the phone and came to me. She said, “Mrs. Costa, has your daughter ever had a fainting spell?” “Oh my God!” I said, “Don’t tell me you’re running for my daughter!” “Yes,” she said, “come on in.” So, I went into the room. And my daughter was on the bed with her bedroom slippers and her housecoat on. And the beads of sweat – honest to God, not to exaggerate, they were that big. It came – just poured from her – I said, “What did you do, kill my daughter? You killed her! You killed her!” That’s what I kept yelling. And the doctor rushed in, see, they were trying to get the doctor, you know. She was a lady doctor. Dr. Schwartz actually did the operation, but she was his assistant, Dr. Montique. She was a lady doctor. So, she came in and she said – I said, “You killed my daughter – what did you do to her?” She said, “We don’t know what happened, Mrs. Costa.” She took me away from the bed. The nurse said to me, “Talk to her.” I said, “Talk to her? My daughter’s dying, what am I supposed to tell her?” And I was screaming. My hair was all pulled out. I went hysterical, you know what I mean? She was only eighteen years old. Beautiful girl, you can see from her picture. And just as I turned my back, my daughter passed away. She was getting ready to go home. I had the clothes and everything ready. My husband was waiting for her. He came home, went upstairs to shave and wash, then sat on the step, waiting. “When is the mother coming?” The hospital kept calling. The people in the office didn’t know what had happened yet. They kept calling the store, trying to reach us. They asked, “When is the mother coming to pick up Marie? She’s discharged.” And Rita said “That’s strange, the mother went to the hospital. She should be there.” So they came back to the hospital to look for us. Rita said, “Gee, that’s strange,” she said, “Mother must’ve gotten really worked up about Marie.” You know, I’ve always had a bad heart. “Mother must’ve gotten so upset about Marie, she probably made herself sick,” Rita thought. So she came over with my son-in-law, in the car. And I was in the main hallway, at the office. I was all pulled apart. One doctor wanted to give me a shot. I said, “Get out of here, I’ll kick you,” I said. “You killed my daughter, now you want to kill me?” Another nurse came over. See, they all knew me. I had three operations in no time, not even a year. And I knew all the nurses. “Mrs. Costa, please, take –” “Get out!” I said, “Don’t you dare come near me.” I was so hysterical. I was banging my head against the wall and everything. They had to grab me. Then they brought me to the office, see. When Rita came in and saw me like that, she asked, “Mother, what happened?” I said, “We lost Marie. Marie died.” Well, that’s this one. This is a picture of Rita. She went down like lightning. Dropped to the floor, and that’s a marble floor. She went down like lightning. And the back of her leg, it felt just like a pole. Hard like a pole, the back of her leg. And that girl is suffering. Thirty – no, twenty-eight years since her sister died? When she gets that pain, it’s like she has phlebitis from it. She suffers – seven, eight, nine weeks at a time. She can’t walk, she goes through such pain since then. And how did they figure how Marie died? The girl who was walking with Marie, told the doctor that Marie was saying goodbye to Arlene. And all Marie said to that girl was, “Oh, I’ve got a pain in my leg and I can’t see.” And then Marie collapsed. Later, they said it was a blood clot. I said, “Don’t you dare touch my daughter. I’ll burn this hospital down! I don’t care who’s in it.” I said, “Don’t you dare touch my daughter. You want to find out what she died from.” But if you touch my daughter, I’ll kill you.” I wouldn’t let them touch her. So when I got home – they took me home. I was screaming in the car. I couldn’t get out, you know, I couldn’t – I was screaming! Everybody came out, coming out of their doors because they could hear the screams. My husband was sitting on the step. He asked, “What’s the matter? What happened to you?” Well, my hair was all pulled, and my face – I was a mess. I didn’t know what I had done to myself. I said, “Mike, we lost Marie. Marie died.” It was just a shock. My husband had a heart attack. Yes, he had a heart attack. Then my son, Michael, yeah. That night. Right there, my Michael came, and my Michael collapsed. He had a heart attack too, no kidding. It was all – my two sisters were here, waiting until about half-past four. They waited because they knew she was coming home that day, waiting for the call. My younger sister had brought an orange for Marie, and I had never seen an orange like it. It was a big orange, like that. She said, “Mary, look, when they call you, when you pick Marie up, let us know, and we’ll come over.” I said, “All right.” So, I got home and called everybody, told them what happened. I called my brother-in-law. He was an undertaker. My sister’s husband – he’s passed away now too. God rest his soul. Maybe you know him... So they were waiting for my call, you know, but instead, I sent my son James, he was little then. I said, “Go tell Aunt Millie and Aunt Jenny.” They had cleaned the pavement, and done the cement work, and they were cleaning the pavement. They said, “Here comes little Jamie,” and now they said, “Maybe he’s come to tell us Marie’s home.” But when he got there, the poor kid’s eyes were all red. They said, “James, what’s the matter, honey? Why are you crying?” “Aunt Jenny, Aunt Millie, Marie died,” he said. Well, my sisters and my mother, you know, they were all alive. It was a shock to everybody. That was a shock that – The neighborhood – Annie at the store always says, “Mrs. Costa,” every time I go in there, she reminds me, “I just picture Marie standing at that step, looking over here.” She used to stand at that step. She was a real quiet girl, pleasant, didn’t talk much, but she was so good, I can’t even tell you. That’s all. So that’s what happened.
summary:
My daughter Marie died twenty-eight years ago this month, on April 30th, after an operation for appendicitis at Methodist Hospital. The surgery was late on a Saturday, and by Sunday morning, she was still under ether. My oldest daughter, Victoria, visited and found the nurses trying to get Marie out of bed, even though she was still very sick. I always blamed them for the blood clot that eventually killed her, thinking it was caused by getting her up too soon.

After ten days in the hospital, I got a call to pick Marie up. I prepared her clothes and coffee, as she had been wishing for it. When I arrived, the nurse asked me to sit down, and I started feeling chills. Suddenly, the nurse asked if Marie had ever fainted, and I knew something was wrong. I was brought into her room, where Marie was sweating heavily and dying. I screamed at the doctors and nurses, blaming them for her death. Marie was only eighteen, beautiful, and about to come home.

My husband was waiting for us at home, and the hospital kept calling the store to check if I had picked her up. When my daughter Rita and my son-in-law arrived at the hospital, they found me hysterical. I told them Marie had died, and Rita collapsed in shock, injuring her leg, which still causes her pain. The doctors later said Marie died from a blood clot, likely caused by getting her out of bed too soon.

When I got home, I was still screaming, and my husband, Mike, had a heart attack from the shock. My son Michael also collapsed that night. My sisters and mother were waiting for news, and my young son James had to tell them Marie had died. The whole neighborhood was shocked, and people still remember Marie standing on the steps, quiet and good. That’s what happened.

summary:
My daughter Marie died twenty-eight years ago this month, on the thirtieth. She was only eighteen. She had an operation for appendicitis at Methodist Hospital, late on a Saturday afternoon. The next morning, my oldest daughter Victoria went to check on her and found her still under ether, but the nurses insisted on getting her out of bed because the doctor ordered it. Victoria protested, saying Marie was too sick, but they tried anyway, and Marie passed out. I always blamed them for the blood clot she developed, thinking it was from taking her out of bed too soon.

After ten days, the hospital called to say Marie was ready to come home. I didn’t have a phone, so the lady from the store called me. I had all her clothes ready, and I told Rita, my other daughter, to finish the cooking and put on a pot of coffee, since Marie had been wishing for coffee. I went to the hospital with her coat and dress, and Marie saw me from the window, saying goodbye to her friends. The hospital told me to bring her girdle and that there would be an extra charge for her board.

When I arrived, the nurse told me to sit down. I started feeling chills and was praying for another woman in the hospital who had ten children and was in bad shape. Suddenly, a nurse’s aide noticed I was going into a cold sweat. The nurse asked if Marie had ever fainted before, and I realized something was wrong. They took me to Marie’s room, where she was lying on the bed, sweating heavily. I started yelling at the doctors and nurses, accusing them of killing my daughter. Dr. Schwartz had done the operation, but his assistant, Dr. Montique, was there. She said they didn’t know what happened. I was hysterical, and just as I turned my back, Marie died.

My husband was at home waiting for us, and the hospital kept calling the store to find out when I would arrive. Rita and my son-in-law came to the hospital, thinking I had gotten sick from worry. When Rita saw me, I told her Marie had died, and she collapsed on the marble floor, injuring her leg. Since then, Rita has suffered from pain in her leg, like phlebitis, for weeks at a time.

Later, we learned that Marie had complained of pain in her leg and said she couldn’t see before collapsing. They said it was a blood clot. I wouldn’t let anyone touch her body until we knew what happened. When I got home, I was screaming, and everyone came out to see what was wrong. I told my husband, Mike, that Marie had died, and he had a heart attack. That night, my son Michael also had a heart attack. My sisters had been waiting for news, and when my young son James told them Marie had died, everyone was in shock. The whole neighborhood mourned her. Marie was a quiet, good girl, and her loss was devastating for all of us.

summary:
My daughter Marie died twenty-eight years ago this month, on the thirtieth. She passed away at Methodist Hospital after an operation for appendicitis. The surgery was late on a Saturday afternoon, and by Sunday morning, my oldest daughter Victoria went to check on her. Marie was still under ether, which they used a lot back then. The nurses were trying to get her out of bed, following the doctor’s orders, even though she was still under anesthesia and too sick. When they tried, she passed out and had to be put back in bed. I always blamed them for the blood clot she developed, thinking it was from taking her out of bed too soon.

After ten days, Marie was supposed to come home. I didn’t have a phone, so the lady from the store called me when the hospital said Marie was discharged. I had all her clothes ready, and I told my daughter Rita to finish the cooking and put on a pot of coffee, since Marie had been wishing for coffee. Every morning since she died, I offer the first cup to her. I also told Rita to get some cake from the bakery. I went to the hospital myself, carrying Marie’s coat and dress. It was chilly, and I had everything ready for her. Marie saw me from the window as I arrived, saying goodbye to her friends.

When I got there, the nurse told me to sit down. While I waited, I prayed for another woman in the hospital who had ten children and was in bad shape after a gall bladder operation. As I sat there, I started getting chills, and a nurse’s aide noticed I was going into a cold sweat. Then the nurse asked if Marie had ever had fainting spells, and I immediately feared something was wrong. They brought me into Marie’s room, where she was lying on the bed in her housecoat and slippers, sweating heavily. I started yelling at the staff, accusing them of killing my daughter. The doctor, Dr. Montique, who assisted Dr. Schwartz in the operation, came in and tried to calm me, but I was hysterical. The nurse asked me to talk to Marie, but I didn’t know what to say. Just as I turned my back, Marie died. She was only eighteen, beautiful, and ready to come home. My husband was waiting for her at home, and the hospital kept calling the store to find out when I would arrive.

Rita and my son-in-law came to the hospital, thinking maybe I had gotten sick from worry. When Rita saw me, I told her Marie had died. Rita collapsed on the marble floor, and ever since, she’s suffered terrible pain in her leg, like phlebitis, lasting weeks at a time. The girl who was with Marie when she collapsed told the doctor that Marie had complained of pain in her leg and said she couldn’t see, then she collapsed. Later, they said it was a blood clot. I wouldn’t let anyone touch Marie’s body, insisting they find out what she died from.

They took me home, but I was screaming in the car, and everyone in the neighborhood came out to see what was happening. My husband was waiting on the steps, and when I told him Marie had died, he had a heart attack. That night, my son Michael also had a heart attack. My sisters had been waiting for news, expecting Marie to come home. My younger sister had brought a huge orange for her. Instead of calling them, I sent my young son James to tell them. When he arrived, his eyes were red from crying, and he told my sisters and mother that Marie had died. It was a shock to everyone, including the whole neighborhood. Annie at the store still remembers Marie standing on the steps, looking over. Marie was a quiet, pleasant girl, very good, and her loss was deeply felt by all of us. That’s what happened.

I said, “And put a big pot of coffee on,” because she had been wishing for coffee. And you know, every morning, that first cup of coffee, I offered it to her. So I said, “Put a pot of coffee on, and send for some cake from the bakery.” I’m going to get Marie; they called me.” “All right, mother, go ahead,” she said, “I’ll finish up.” And I went myself. You know, I had the coat on my arm. She was operated on in March – No, April! So that was the thirtieth. She was operated – ten days before, so that would be the twentieth. The twentieth of April, right? It was kind of chilly. Oh, and I had everything in a bag, and I had the coat on my arm and her dress hanging on my arm. She saw me from the windows, from Wolf Street. She said, “My mother’s just coming.” She was with the girls, saying goodbye to everyone – you know how it is. They told me on the phone, it would be ten dollars more for her board. And to make sure I brought her girdle to put on. I said, “All right, I’ll be right over.” And I went. But when I got there, the nurse said to me, “Sit down, Mrs. Costa, sit here for a while.” So I sat down, and I was praying for another lady in the hospital that had an operation for her gall bladder. She lived near my sister, and she had ten children. They had said she was in very bad shape. I prayed, “Oh dear God, don’t take that mother away from her children.” I was praying for her, you know. Then a little nurse’s aide went to the closet and got some blankets. I started getting the chills, and I was thinking about it, when the aide said to the nurse, “Oh, she’s going into a cold sweat now.” The nurse hung up the phone and came to me. She said, “Mrs. Costa, has your daughter ever had a fainting spell?” “Oh my God!” I said, “Don’t tell me you’re running for my daughter!” “Yes,” she said, “come on in.” So, I went into the room. And my daughter was on the bed with her bedroom slippers and her housecoat on. And the beads of sweat – honest to God, not to exaggerate, they were that big. It came – just poured from her – I said, “What did you do, kill my daughter? You killed her! You killed her!” That’s what I kept yelling. She was a lady doctor. So, she came in and she said – I said, “You killed my daughter – what did you do to her?” She said, “We don’t know what happened, Mrs. Costa.” She took me away from the bed. The nurse said to me, “Talk to her.” I said, “Talk to her? My daughter’s dying, what am I supposed to tell her?” And I was screaming. My hair was all pulled out. I went hysterical, you know what I mean? She was only eighteen years old. Beautiful girl, you can see from her picture. And just as I turned my back, my daughter passed away. She was getting ready to go home. I had the clothes and everything ready. My husband was waiting for her. He came home, went upstairs to shave and wash, then sat on the step, waiting. “When is the mother coming?” The hospital kept calling. The people in the office didn’t know what had happened yet. They kept calling the store, trying to reach us. They asked, “When is the mother coming to pick up Marie? She’s discharged.” And Rita said “That’s strange, the mother went to the hospital. She should be there.” So they came back to the hospital to look for us. Rita said, “Gee, that’s strange,” she said, “Mother must’ve gotten really worked up about Marie.” You know, I’ve always had a bad heart. “Mother must’ve gotten so upset about Marie, she probably made herself sick,” Rita thought. So she came over with my son-in-law, in the car. And I was in the main hallway, at the office. I was all pulled apart. One doctor wanted to give me a shot. I said, “Get out of here, I’ll kick you,” I said. “You killed my daughter, now you want to kill me?” Another nurse came over. See, they all knew me. I had three operations in no time, not even a year. And I knew all the nurses. “Mrs. Costa, please, take –” “Get out!” I said, “Don’t you dare come near me.” I was so hysterical. I was banging my head against the wall and everything. They had to grab me. Then they brought me to the office, see. When Rita came in and saw me like that, she asked, “Mother, what happened?” I said, “We lost Marie. Marie died.” Well, that’s this one. This is a picture of Rita. She went down like lightning. Dropped to the floor, and that’s a marble floor. She went down like lightning. And the back of her leg, it felt just like a pole. Hard like a pole, the back of her leg. And that girl is suffering. Thirty – no, twenty-eight years since her sister died? When she gets that pain, it’s like she has phlebitis from it. She suffers – seven, eight, nine weeks at a time. She can’t walk, she goes through such pain since then. And how did they figure how Marie died? The girl who was walking with Marie, told the doctor that Marie was saying goodbye to Arlene. And all Marie said to that girl was, “Oh, I’ve got a pain in my leg and I can’t see.” And then Marie collapsed. Later, they said it was a blood clot. I said, “Don’t you dare touch my daughter. I’ll burn this hospital down! I don’t care who’s in it.” I said, “Don’t you dare touch my daughter. You want to find out what she died from.” But if you touch my daughter, I’ll kill you.” I wouldn’t let them touch her. So when I got home – they took me home. I was screaming in the car. I couldn’t get out, you know, I couldn’t – I was screaming! Everybody came out, coming out of their doors because they could hear the screams. My husband was sitting on the step. He asked, “What’s the matter? What happened to you?” Well, my hair was all pulled, and my face – I was a mess. I didn’t know what I had done to myself. I said, “Mike, we lost Marie. Marie died.” It was just a shock. My husband had a heart attack. Yes, he had a heart attack. Then my son, Michael, yeah. That night. Right there, my Michael came, and my Michael collapsed. He had a heart attack too, no kidding. It was all – my two sisters were here, waiting until about half-past four. They waited because they knew she was coming home that day, waiting for the call. My younger sister had brought an orange for Marie, and I had never seen an orange like it. It was a big orange, like that. She said, “Mary, look, when they call you, when you pick Marie up, let us know, and we’ll come over.” I said, “All right.” So, I got home and called everybody, told them what happened. I called my brother-in-law. He was an undertaker. My sister’s husband – he’s passed away now too. God rest his soul. Maybe you know him... So they were waiting for my call, you know, but instead, I sent my son James, he was little then. I said, “Go tell Aunt Millie and Aunt Jenny.” They had cleaned the pavement, and done the cement work, and they were cleaning the pavement. They said, “Here comes little Jamie,” and now they said, “Maybe he’s come to tell us Marie’s home.” But when he got there, the poor kid’s eyes were all red. They said, “James, what’s the matter, honey? Why are you crying?” “Aunt Jenny, Aunt Millie, Marie died,” he said. Well, my sisters and my mother, you know, they were all alive. It was a shock to everybody. That was a shock that – The neighborhood – Annie at the store always says, “Mrs. Costa,” every time I go in there, she reminds me, “I just picture Marie standing at that step, looking over here.” She used to stand at that step. She was a real quiet girl, pleasant, didn’t talk much, but she was so good, I can’t even tell you. That’s all. So that’s what happened.

So I said, “Put a pot of coffee on, and send for some cake from the bakery.” I’m going to get Marie; they called me.” “All right, mother, go ahead,” she said, “I’ll finish up.” And I went myself. You know, I had the coat on my arm. She was operated on in March – No, April! So that was the thirtieth. She was operated – ten days before, so that would be the twentieth. The twentieth of April, right? It was kind of chilly. Oh, and I had everything in a bag, and I had the coat on my arm and her dress hanging on my arm. She saw me from the windows, from Wolf Street. She said, “My mother’s just coming.” She was with the girls, saying goodbye to everyone – you know how it is. They told me on the phone, it would be ten dollars more for her board. And to make sure I brought her girdle to put on. I said, “All right, I’ll be right over.” And I went. But when I got there, the nurse said to me, “Sit down, Mrs. Costa, sit here for a while.” So I sat down, and I was praying for another lady in the hospital that had an operation for her gall bladder. She lived near my sister, and she had ten children. They had said she was in very bad shape. I prayed, “Oh dear God, don’t take that mother away from her children.” I was praying for her, you know. Then a little nurse’s aide went to the closet and got some blankets. I started getting the chills, and I was thinking about it, when the aide said to the nurse, “Oh, she’s going into a cold sweat now.” The nurse hung up the phone and came to me. She said, “Mrs. Costa, has your daughter ever had a fainting spell?” “Oh my God!” I said, “Don’t tell me you’re running for my daughter!” “Yes,” she said, “come on in.” So, I went into the room. And my daughter was on the bed with her bedroom slippers and her housecoat on. And the beads of sweat – honest to God, not to exaggerate, they were that big. It came – just poured from her – I said, “What did you do, kill my daughter? You killed her! You killed her!” That’s what I kept yelling. And the doctor rushed in, see, they were trying to get the doctor, you know. She was a lady doctor. Dr. Schwartz actually did the operation, but she was his assistant, Dr. Montique. She was a lady doctor. So, she came in and she said – I said, “You killed my daughter – what did you do to her?” She said, “We don’t know what happened, Mrs. Costa.” She took me away from the bed. The nurse said to me, “Talk to her.” I said, “Talk to her? My daughter’s dying, what am I supposed to tell her?” And I was screaming. My hair was all pulled out. I went hysterical, you know what I mean? She was only eighteen years old. Beautiful girl, you can see from her picture. And just as I turned my back, my daughter passed away. She was getting ready to go home. I had the clothes and everything ready. My husband was waiting for her. He came home, went upstairs to shave and wash, then sat on the step, waiting. “When is the mother coming?” The hospital kept calling. The people in the office didn’t know what had happened yet. They kept calling the store, trying to reach us. They asked, “When is the mother coming to pick up Marie? She’s discharged.” And Rita said “That’s strange, the mother went to the hospital. She should be there.” So they came back to the hospital to look for us. Rita said, “Gee, that’s strange,” she said, “Mother must’ve gotten really worked up about Marie.” You know, I’ve always had a bad heart. “Mother must’ve gotten so upset about Marie, she probably made herself sick,” Rita thought. So she came over with my son-in-law, in the car. And I was in the main hallway, at the office. I was all pulled apart. One doctor wanted to give me a shot. I said, “Get out of here, I’ll kick you,” I said. “You killed my daughter, now you want to kill me?” Another nurse came over. See, they all knew me. I had three operations in no time, not even a year. And I knew all the nurses. “Mrs. Costa, please, take –” “Get out!” I said, “Don’t you dare come near me.” I was so hysterical. I was banging my head against the wall and everything. They had to grab me. Then they brought me to the office, see. When Rita came in and saw me like that, she asked, “Mother, what happened?” I said, “We lost Marie. Marie died.” Well, that’s this one. This is a picture of Rita. She went down like lightning. Dropped to the floor, and that’s a marble floor. She went down like lightning. And the back of her leg, it felt just like a pole. Hard like a pole, the back of her leg. And that girl is suffering. Thirty – no, twenty-eight years since her sister died? When she gets that pain, it’s like she has phlebitis from it. She suffers – seven, eight, nine weeks at a time. She can’t walk, she goes through such pain since then. And how did they figure how Marie died? The girl who was walking with Marie, told the doctor that Marie was saying goodbye to Arlene. And all Marie said to that girl was, “Oh, I’ve got a pain in my leg and I can’t see.” And then Marie collapsed. Later, they said it was a blood clot. I said, “Don’t you dare touch my daughter. I’ll burn this hospital down! I don’t care who’s in it.” I said, “Don’t you dare touch my daughter. You want to find out what she died from.” But if you touch my daughter, I’ll kill you.” I wouldn’t let them touch her. So when I got home – they took me home. I was screaming in the car. I couldn’t get out, you know, I couldn’t – I was screaming! Everybody came out, coming out of their doors because they could hear the screams. My husband was sitting on the step. He asked, “What’s the matter? What happened to you?” Well, my hair was all pulled, and my face – I was a mess. I didn’t know what I had done to myself. I said, “Mike, we lost Marie. Marie died.” It was just a shock. My husband had a heart attack. Yes, he had a heart attack. Then my son, Michael, yeah. That night. Right there, my Michael came, and my Michael collapsed. He had a heart attack too, no kidding. It was all – my two sisters were here, waiting until about half-past four. They waited because they knew she was coming home that day, waiting for the call. My younger sister had brought an orange for Marie, and I had never seen an orange like it. It was a big orange, like that. She said, “Mary, look, when they call you, when you pick Marie up, let us know, and we’ll come over.” I said, “All right.” So, I got home and called everybody, told them what happened. I called my brother-in-law. He was an undertaker. My sister’s husband – he’s passed away now too. God rest his soul. Maybe you know him... So they were waiting for my call, you know, but instead, I sent my son James, he was little then. I said, “Go tell Aunt Millie and Aunt Jenny.” They had cleaned the pavement, and done the cement work, and they were cleaning the pavement. They said, “Here comes little Jamie,” and now they said, “Maybe he’s come to tell us Marie’s home.” But when he got there, the poor kid’s eyes were all red. They said, “James, what’s the matter, honey? Why are you crying?” “Aunt Jenny, Aunt Millie, Marie died,” he said. Well, my sisters and my mother, you know, they were all alive. It was a shock to everybody. That was a shock that – The neighborhood – Annie at the store always says, “Mrs. Costa,” every time I go in there, she reminds me, “I just picture Marie standing at that step, looking over here.” She used to stand at that step. She was a real quiet girl, pleasant, didn’t talk much, but she was so good, I can’t even tell you. That’s all. So that’s what happened.
summary:
My daughter Marie died twenty-eight years ago this month, on the thirtieth. She passed away at Methodist Hospital after an operation for appendicitis. The surgery was late on a Saturday afternoon, and by Sunday morning, my oldest daughter Victoria went to check on her. Marie was still under ether, which they used a lot back then. There was a chair in the room, and Victoria asked the nurse about it. The nurse said they needed to get Marie out of bed, as per the doctor’s orders, even though she was still under ether and too sick. When they tried to get her up, she passed out and had to be put back in bed. I always blamed them for the blood clot she developed, thinking it was from taking her out of bed too soon.

After ten days, Marie was supposed to come home. I was waiting for the call from the hospital, but I didn’t have a phone, so the lady from the store called me at a quarter to five in the evening. She told me the hospital had called and Marie was discharged. I had all her clothes laid out, and I was cooking. I told my daughter Rita to finish the cooking and put on a big pot of coffee, since Marie had been wishing for coffee. Every morning since she died, I offer the first cup of coffee to her. I also told Rita to send for some cake from the bakery. I got everything ready—her coat, her dress, and a bag with her things—and went to the hospital myself.

It was chilly, being April, and as I walked to the hospital, Marie saw me from the window, telling the girls she was saying goodbye to that her mother was coming. The hospital had told me on the phone that it would be ten dollars more for her board and to bring her girdle. When I arrived, the nurse told me to sit down for a while. While I waited, I prayed for another woman in the hospital who had ten children and was in bad shape after a gall bladder operation. As I sat there, I started getting chills, and a nurse’s aide noticed I was going into a cold sweat. Then the nurse asked if Marie had ever had a fainting spell, and I immediately feared something was wrong.

They brought me into the room, and Marie was on the bed in her housecoat and slippers, beads of sweat pouring from her. I started yelling, asking if they had killed my daughter. The doctor rushed in—Dr. Schwartz had done the operation, but his assistant, Dr. Montique, was there. She said they didn’t know what had happened. The nurse told me to talk to Marie, but I was hysterical, screaming, and pulling my hair. Marie was only eighteen, a beautiful girl. Just as I turned my back, she died.

We had everything ready for her to come home. My husband was waiting, sitting on the step after coming home from work. The hospital kept calling the store, trying to reach us, asking when I would come to pick up Marie. Rita thought maybe I had gotten sick from worry, so she and my son-in-law drove to the hospital. I was in the main hallway, completely hysterical, refusing shots or help from the doctors and nurses I knew from my own previous operations. When Rita arrived and saw me, I told her Marie had died. Rita collapsed on the marble floor, and since then, she has suffered terrible pain in her leg, like phlebitis, for weeks at a time.

The girl who was with Marie when she collapsed told the doctor that Marie had said goodbye to Arlene and then complained of pain in her leg and that she couldn’t see. Then she collapsed. Later, they said it was a blood clot. I wouldn’t let anyone touch her, demanding to know what she died from and threatening the hospital if they did anything to her body.

They took me home, but I was screaming in the car, and everyone in the neighborhood came out to see what was happening. My husband was sitting on the step, and when I told him Marie had died, he had a heart attack. That night, my son Michael also had a heart attack. My two sisters had been waiting at my house for the call that Marie was coming home. My younger sister had brought a huge orange for her. When I got home, I called everyone to tell them what happened, including my brother-in-law, who was an undertaker. I sent my young son James to tell my sisters, and when he arrived, they thought he was bringing good news, but he told them Marie had died. It was a shock to everyone—my sisters, my mother, the whole neighborhood.

Annie at the store always remembers Marie standing at the step, looking over. Marie was a quiet, pleasant girl, very good, and I can’t even describe how wonderful she was. That’s what happened.

They said, “James, what’s the matter, honey? Why are you crying?” “Aunt Jenny, Aunt Millie, Marie died,” he said. Well, my sisters and my mother, you know, they were all alive. summary:
My daughter died twenty-eight years ago this month, on the thirtieth. She passed away at Methodist Hospital after an operation for appendicitis. The surgery was late on a Saturday afternoon, and by Sunday morning, my oldest daughter, Victoria, went to check on her. Marie was still under ether, which they used a lot back then. There was a chair in the room, and Victoria asked the nurse about it. The nurse said they needed to get Marie out of bed, as per the doctor’s orders, even though she was still under the effects of ether and too sick. When they tried to get her up, she passed out and had to be put back in bed. I always blamed them for the blood clot she developed, thinking it was from taking her out of bed too soon.

After ten days, Marie was supposed to come home. I was waiting for the call from the hospital, but I didn’t have a phone, so the lady from the store called me at a quarter to five in the evening. She told me the hospital had called and that Marie was discharged. I had all her clothes laid out on the table, and I was cooking. I told Rita, my other daughter, to finish the cooking and put on a big pot of coffee because Marie had been wishing for coffee. Every morning since she died, I offer the first cup of coffee to her. I also told Rita to send for some cake from the bakery. I got everything ready—her coat, her dress, and a bag with her things—and went to the hospital myself.

It was chilly, being April, and I remember carrying her coat and dress on my arm. Marie saw me from the window as I arrived, saying to her friends that her mother was coming. She was saying goodbye to everyone. The hospital had told me on the phone that it would be ten dollars more for her board and to bring her girdle. When I got there, the nurse told me to sit down for a while. While I waited, I prayed for another woman in the hospital who had a gall bladder operation and ten children, asking God not to take her away from her family.

As I sat there, I started getting chills, and a nurse’s aide commented that I was going into a cold sweat. Then the nurse asked if Marie had ever had a fainting spell, which alarmed me. She told me to come with her, and I went into the room to find Marie on the bed in her slippers and housecoat, sweating profusely. I started yelling, asking what they had done to my daughter. The doctor rushed in—it was Dr. Montique, the assistant to Dr. Schwartz, who had done the operation. She said they didn’t know what had happened. The nurse told me to talk to Marie, but I was hysterical, screaming, and pulling my hair. Marie was only eighteen, a beautiful girl. Just as I turned my back, she died.

We had everything ready for her to come home. My husband was waiting, having come home from work, cleaned up, and sat on the step waiting for us. The hospital kept calling the store, trying to reach us, not knowing what had happened. Rita thought maybe I had gotten sick from worry and came to the hospital with my son-in-law. When they arrived, I was in the main hallway, completely distraught. The doctors and nurses tried to calm me, but I was hysterical, banging my head against the wall, and they had to restrain me. Rita came in and asked what happened, and I told her we lost Marie. She collapsed on the marble floor, and since then, she has suffered terrible pain in her leg, like phlebitis, for weeks at a time.

Later, we learned from the girl who was with Marie that as she was saying goodbye to her friend Arlene, she complained of pain in her leg and said she couldn’t see, then collapsed. They said it was a blood clot. I was furious and wouldn’t let anyone touch Marie, insisting they find out what she died from before doing anything.

They took me home, but I was screaming in the car, unable to control myself. The neighbors came out because they could hear me. My husband was sitting on the step, and when he saw me, he asked what happened. I told him we lost Marie, and he had a heart attack from the shock. That night, my son Michael also had a heart attack. My two sisters had been waiting at my house for news, expecting Marie to come home. My younger sister had brought a huge orange for her. When I got home, I called everyone to tell them what happened, including my brother-in-law, who was an undertaker.

I sent my young son James to tell my sisters, Aunt Millie and Aunt Jenny, who were cleaning the pavement. When they saw James coming, they thought he was bringing good news, but when he arrived, his eyes were red from crying, and he told them Marie had died. It was a shock to everyone—my sisters, my mother, the whole neighborhood. Annie at the store still remembers Marie standing at the step, looking over. Marie was a quiet, pleasant girl, very good, and her loss was deeply felt by all of us. That’s what happened.

summary:
It’s been nearly twenty-eight years since my daughter Marie died, and I remember every detail as if it happened yesterday. She passed away on the thirtieth of April, after an operation for appendicitis at Methodist Hospital. The operation was late on a Saturday afternoon, and by Sunday morning, my oldest daughter Victoria went to check on her. Marie was still under ether, which they used a lot back then. There was a chair in the room, and Victoria asked the nurse about it. The nurse said they needed to get Marie out of bed, as per the doctor’s orders, even though Victoria protested that Marie was still under ether and too sick. They tried to get her up, but she passed out and had to be put back in bed. I always blamed them for the blood clot she developed, thinking it was from taking her out of bed too soon.

After ten days in the hospital, Marie was finally ready to come home. I didn’t have a phone, so the lady from the store called me at a quarter to five in the evening to say the hospital had called and Marie was being discharged. I had all her clothes laid out on the table, and I was cooking when I got the news. I told Rita, my other daughter, to finish the cooking and put on a big pot of coffee, because Marie had been wishing for coffee. Every morning since she died, I offer the first cup of coffee to her. I also told Rita to send for some cake from the bakery, so we could celebrate Marie’s return.

It was chilly that day, so I brought Marie’s coat and dress with me to the hospital. She saw me from the window as I arrived, saying goodbye to her friends. The hospital had told me on the phone that it would be ten dollars more for her board and to make sure I brought her girdle. When I got there, the nurse told me to sit down for a while. As I waited, I prayed for another woman in the hospital who had ten children and was in bad shape after a gall bladder operation. I prayed that God wouldn’t take that mother away from her children.

While I was sitting there, a nurse’s aide noticed I was getting chills and said I was going into a cold sweat. The nurse then asked me if Marie had ever had a fainting spell. I immediately feared the worst and asked if something was wrong with my daughter. The nurse told me to come with her, and I found Marie on the bed in her housecoat and slippers, beads of sweat pouring from her. I started yelling at the staff, accusing them of killing my daughter. The doctor rushed in—Dr. Schwartz had done the operation, but his assistant, Dr. Montique, was there. She told me they didn’t know what had happened. The nurse asked me to talk to Marie, but I was hysterical, screaming and pulling my hair. Marie was only eighteen years old, a beautiful girl, and just as I turned my back, she passed away.

We had everything ready for her at home. My husband was waiting, having come home from work, cleaned up, and sat on the step waiting for us. The hospital kept calling the store, trying to reach us, asking when I would come to pick up Marie. Rita thought it was strange that I hadn’t returned yet and assumed I must have gotten upset and made myself sick. She and my son-in-law drove to the hospital, where they found me in the main hallway, completely distraught. The doctors and nurses tried to calm me down, but I was hysterical, banging my head against the wall and refusing any help. When Rita arrived and saw me like that, she asked what had happened, and I told her we had lost Marie. Rita collapsed on the marble floor, and ever since then, she has suffered from severe pain in her leg, almost like phlebitis, lasting for weeks at a time.

The doctors later determined that Marie had died from a blood clot. The girl who had been walking with Marie told the doctor that Marie had complained of pain in her leg and said she couldn’t see, then collapsed. I was furious and wouldn’t let anyone touch her body, insisting they find out what she died from before doing anything else.

They took me home, but I was screaming in the car, unable to control myself. When we arrived, everyone in the neighborhood came out to see what was happening because they could hear my screams. My husband was sitting on the step, waiting for us. When he saw me, he asked what had happened, and I told him we had lost Marie. The shock was too much for him, and he had a heart attack right there. That night, my son Michael also collapsed and had a heart attack. My two sisters had been waiting at my house, expecting Marie to come home. My younger sister had brought a huge orange for Marie, and they were waiting for my call. Instead, I sent my young son James to tell them what had happened. When he arrived, his eyes were red from crying, and he told his aunts that Marie had died. The news was a shock to everyone—my sisters, my mother, and the whole neighborhood.

Annie at the store still remembers Marie standing on the step, looking over at her. Marie was a quiet, pleasant girl who didn’t talk much but was very good. That’s the story of what happened to my daughter Marie.

Marie’s death affected the whole family deeply. My husband’s heart attack was the first sign of how devastating the loss was. He had been waiting for her to come home, sitting on the step, and the shock of losing her was too much for him. My son Michael’s heart attack that same night added to the tragedy. Rita’s collapse at the hospital and her ongoing pain in her leg have been a constant reminder of that day. Even now, decades later, she suffers for weeks at a time, unable to walk because of the pain that started when her sister died.

The hospital staff tried to comfort me, but I couldn’t accept their help. I was angry and blamed them for Marie’s death, especially for taking her out of bed too soon after her operation. I refused to let them touch her body, demanding answers about what had happened. The doctors said it was a blood clot, but I always believed it was caused by their actions.

The neighborhood was close-knit, and everyone knew each other. When I got home, the neighbors came out because they heard my screams. My sisters and mother were devastated by the news. My younger sister had brought an orange for Marie, and they had been waiting for her to come home. Instead, they learned of her death from my son James, who was just a child at the time.

Marie was a quiet, good girl who didn’t talk much but was always pleasant. Annie at the store still remembers her standing on the step, looking over at the store. The memory of Marie is still strong in the neighborhood, even after all these years.

Since Marie’s death, I have kept her memory alive in small ways. Every morning, I offer the first cup of coffee to her, just as I did when she was alive. The pain of losing her has never gone away, and the impact on my family has been profound. My husband and son both suffered heart attacks from the shock, and Rita continues to suffer physically from the trauma of that day.

The events of that day are etched in my memory. From the moment I got the call to pick up Marie, to the preparations at home, to the shock and chaos at the hospital, and finally the devastation when I returned home, every detail is still vivid. The loss of Marie changed our family forever, and her absence is still felt every day.

Marie’s death was a tragedy that affected not just our family but the entire neighborhood. The support and memories of friends and neighbors have helped keep her memory alive, but the pain of losing her has never faded. That’s what happened to my daughter Marie.

The nurse said to me, “Talk to her.” I said, “Talk to her? My daughter’s dying, what am I supposed to tell her?” And I was screaming. My hair was all pulled out.
